Name: information_bottleneck_model_compression
Title: Exploring the Information Bottleneck for Model Compression: A Rate-Distortion Approach
Short Hypothesis: The use of the information bottleneck principle can provide new insights into optimizing model compression schemes by framing them as a rate-distortion trade-off, potentially leading to tighter bounds on the compression rates while maintaining predictive performance.
Related Work: Previous work has explored rate-distortion bounds in the context of model compression and generalization. Notably, Sefidgaran et al. (2022) established generalization bounds using rate-distortion theory, which can be extended to model compression. However, the specific application of the information bottleneck principle to derive new compression strategies and operational bounds remains underexplored, making this proposal distinct.
Abstract: This proposal seeks to leverage the information bottleneck principle within the framework of model compression to derive new rate-distortion bounds that account for task-relevant distortion. By analyzing the trade-offs between model complexity and predictive performance, we aim to provide a theoretical foundation for achieving optimal compression rates. Specifically, we will formulate the compression problem in terms of mutual information and derive achievable rates under various distortion measures. This work will not only contribute to the understanding of model compressibility but also provide practical guidelines for designing efficient compression algorithms that align with information-theoretic limits. We will validate our findings through a series of experiments that compare traditional compression methods with our proposed framework, measuring the resulting predictive performance against theoretical bounds.
Experiments: Implement a model compression algorithm based on the information bottleneck principle and compare its performance against baseline methods (e.g., pruning, quantization).
Evaluate the compression rates and predictive performance on standard datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, MNIST) while varying distortion measures.
Conduct ablation studies to analyze the impact of different constraints on the mutual information and distortion trade-off.
Use metrics such as bit-per-weight ratios, predictive accuracy, and task-relevant distortion metrics to assess performance.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Theoretical bounds may not fully translate into practical performance due to implementation challenges.
The choice of distortion measure could significantly affect the results and requires careful consideration.
Computational complexity may increase with the proposed methods, potentially limiting scalability.
